articles.title: A System in Motion: The Evolution of Orthopaedic Care in Romania.
articles.authors: Dragosloveanu S, Nedelea DG, Scheau C
articles.population: Orthopaedic patients in Romania
articles.outcomes: Digital infrastructure adoption, robotic-assisted surgery implementation, artificial intelligence applications, hospital bed capacity, arthroplasty register modernisation
articles.take_home: Romania is improving its orthopaedic care system by adopting new technologies like robotic surgery and artificial intelligence, whilst maintaining strong hospital capacity for elderly and complex patients. The country has modernised its joint replacement register to match standards in other European countries.

articles.title: Salvage total hip arthroplasty after internal fixation compared with acute total hip arthroplasty for fracture: a cohort study based on 32,960 cases from the Dutch Arthroplasty Register.
articles.authors: Van Marle L, Peters RM, Van Steenbergen LN, Jaarsma RL, Doornberg JN, Zijlstra WP
articles.study_type: Comparative cohort study using registry data
articles.population: Adults who had total hip replacement for hip fracture
articles.sample_size: 32,960 procedures (12,486 salvage replacements after failed internal fixation, 18,450 acute replacements)
articles.outcomes: Revision rates at 1 and 5 years; risk factors for needing further surgery
articles.take_home: Patients who had a hip replacement after a previous internal fixation attempt had similar revision rates to those who had replacement as a first treatment, suggesting that failed internal fixation does not make the replacement surgery less likely to succeed. However, factors like higher BMI, poorer general health, and certain implant choices increased revision risk in both groups.

articles.title: Treatment and outcomes of fractures in children: a registry-based cohort study of 10,144 fractures.
articles.authors: Laaksonen T, Grahn P, Ahonen M, Ahola JA, Helenius I, Nietosvaara Y
articles.population: Children with fractures treated at a single Level I trauma centre
articles.outcomes: Surgical timing, treatment methods, complication rates, unplanned reoperations, permanent nerve injuries, and deep infections
articles.take_home: Most children's fractures were treated safely with very few nerve injuries or infections. Most forearm fractures were fixed in the emergency department without needing a general anaesthetic, and surgery for more serious fractures like upper arm and thigh bone breaks usually happened within 18 hours.

articles.title: Reliability and clinical utility of paediatric ankle fracture classifications : a systematic review.
articles.authors: Teo LS, Xiao L, Yeo BKK, Rayapati R, Lim AKS, Hoipo Hui J, et al.
articles.study_type: Systematic review of paediatric ankle fracture classification systems
articles.population: Studies evaluating reliability and clinical utility of paediatric ankle fracture classifications
articles.outcomes: Interobserver and intraobserver reliability of classification systems; ability to predict premature physeal closure and angular deformity
articles.take_home: The Dias-Tachdjian classification was most reliable for orthopaedic surgeons to use consistently, whilst the Salter-Harris classification was most commonly used and best at predicting problems with bone growth. Using both classification types together may help doctors plan better treatment.

articles.title: Diagnostic value of serum troponin in stable patients at risk for blunt cardiac injury.
articles.authors: Cleary HL, Bernard MD, Davenport DL, Bernard AC
articles.population: Adult trauma patients with blunt cardiac injury or sternal fracture who were haemodynamically stable
articles.outcomes: Adverse cardiac events (new arrhythmia requiring treatment, cardiac surgery, or cardiac-related death)
articles.take_home: In stable patients admitted with suspected blunt cardiac injury, troponin levels did not help predict who would have serious heart problems, suggesting that troponin testing may not be necessary in this group of patients.

articles.title: The fate of stage I Masquelet cement spacers in long bone reconstruction for acute fractures.
articles.authors: Dekle JK, Sisk CK, Litten RM, Alcaide DM, McIlwain RN, Wilson AL, et al.
articles.population: Adult patients who underwent Masquelet reconstruction for acute long bone fractures at a Level I trauma centre
articles.sample_size: 156 patients (48 Stage I only, 108 completed Stage II)
articles.outcomes: Stage II completion rates, fracture-related infection, hardware removal, wound dehiscence, amputation, and mortality
articles.take_home: About one-third of patients who had the first stage of the Masquelet technique did not go on to have the second stage, most commonly because they required amputation. Among patients followed for at least 6 months, outcomes like infection and amputation rates were similar whether or not they completed both stages.

articles.title: Efficacy and limitations of a web-based native adult knee septic arthritis calculator in urban trauma care.
articles.authors: Serbin RP, Burger J, Odum S, Chandler C, Welch S, Di Napoli N, et al.
articles.population: Adults with suspected knee septic arthritis undergoing arthrocentesis at a tertiary referral centre
articles.outcomes: Diagnostic accuracy of web-based calculator using Newman criteria as gold standard; optimal risk thresholds for surgical intervention and excluding septic arthritis
articles.take_home: A web-based calculator using six clinical and laboratory variables accurately identified septic knee arthritis, with a probability score of 58% or higher recommending surgery and below 24.6% confidently ruling out infection. The tool correctly classified 93% of cases and may help diagnose this challenging condition more reliably in hospital settings.

articles.title: External validation of machine learning models for estimation of mortality 1, 3, 6, and 12 months after hip fracture on 5,055 consecutive patients.
articles.authors: Mosfeldt M, Jorgensen HL, Lauritzen JB, Jansson KA
articles.study_type: External validation study of machine learning models
articles.population: Patients with hip fractures
articles.sample_size: 5,055 consecutive patients from 2 hospitals in Stockholm, Sweden, 2010-2020
articles.outcomes: Mortality at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months after hip fracture; model performance assessed by area under the receiver operating characteristic curve, calibration metrics, and decision curve analysis
articles.take_home: Machine learning models previously developed to predict death after hip fracture performed well when tested on a new group of patients, with the XGB model being most accurate. The models have been adjusted and made available online to help clinicians estimate individual patient survival risk and support treatment decisions.

articles.title: CT-based micromotion analysis for tracking fracture displacement and fragment movement in trochanteric hip fractures: an agreement study.
articles.authors: Aberg H, Hanni M, Sandberg O, Jonsson KB
articles.population: Patients with trochanteric hip fractures treated with cephalomedullary nail
articles.outcomes: Measurement agreement and repeatability of CT-based micromotion analysis for quantifying fracture displacement, surgical reduction, and postoperative fragment movement
articles.take_home: CT-based micromotion analysis can accurately measure hip fracture displacement in three dimensions with excellent repeatability, showing that surgery significantly improved fracture alignment and fragments remained stable during healing.

articles.title: Fear of reinjury is associated with failure to return to preinjury level of activity after operative repair of ankle fractures.
articles.authors: Hadley SM Jr, Peabody JJ, Durudogan EH, Westvold SJ, Bergman R, Chang S, et al.
articles.population: Patients who underwent operative ankle fracture repair at a single academic medical centre between 2016 and 2021
articles.outcomes: Association between fear of reinjury and failure to return to preinjury level of activity
articles.take_home: All patients who experienced fear of reinjury after ankle fracture surgery failed to return to their preinjury activity level, compared with only 12% of those without fear. Fear of reinjury appears to be a significant barrier to recovery after ankle fracture surgery, particularly for those who were more active before their injury.

articles.title: What is the desired screw trajectory for fixation of noncontiguous posterior distal articular fractures associated with distal tibial shaft fractures?
articles.authors: Moon TJ, Blumenschein LA, Swetz AM, Nedder VJ, Ochenjele G, Wetzel RJ, et al.
articles.population: Patients with distal tibial shaft fractures and associated posterior distal articular fractures
articles.sample_size: 56 posterior distal articular fractures in 74 patients
articles.outcomes: Optimal screw trajectory angles (axial and sagittal) for perpendicular fixation and centre engagement of posterior fracture fragments
articles.take_home: There is no single standard screw angle that works well for all patients with this type of fracture. Surgeons should use computed tomography scans before surgery to plan the best screw pathway for each individual patient.

articles.title: CTA-detected incidence and descriptive fracture patterns of arterial injury in 539 surgically treated tibial plateau fractures.
articles.authors: Valderrama J, Narvaez F, Cancino J, Raffo JF, Bosselin R, Zamora F, et al.
articles.population: Patients with surgically treated tibial plateau fractures
articles.outcomes: Incidence of arterial injury detected on CTA; fracture patterns associated with arterial injury
articles.take_home: Arterial injury was found in 1.11% of surgically treated tibial plateau fractures. Certain fracture patterns appeared more common in cases with arterial injury, but these findings are based on very few cases and need further research to confirm.

articles.title: Risk Factors for Failure of Repair of Diaphyseal Tibial Nonunions: A Retrospective Cohort Study of 108 Patients.
articles.authors: Fan S, Wagner RK, Lehle CH, Musick AN, Gregg AT, Janssen SJ, et al.
articles.population: Adults aged 18 or over who had surgery to repair a broken tibia bone that had failed to heal
articles.outcomes: Need for additional surgery to fix persistent nonunion, and whether the bone healed on X-rays. One quarter of patients needed further surgery, and 5 patients had amputation. Smoking, initial open fracture, and bone defects increased the risk of needing more surgery.
articles.take_home: About 1 in 4 patients needed another operation because their tibia still would not heal after initial repair surgery. However, 9 in 10 patients eventually achieved bone healing on X-rays, and smoking cessation before surgery may improve outcomes.

articles.title: Rethinking the Lisfranc injury: Rationale for and development of a new classification system for Lisfranc injuries.
articles.authors: Cawley WCF, Hwang P, Mittal R, Zochowski Y, Darwish I, O'Keefe R, et al.
articles.study_type: Development and validation of a new classification system using retrospective case series data
articles.population: Patients with indirect Lisfranc injuries (midfoot injuries between the cuneiforms and metatarsals)
articles.sample_size: 176 indirect Lisfranc injuries reviewed by three surgeons
articles.outcomes: Inter-observer and intra-observer reliability of the new Suthersan classification compared to the existing Myerson-Hardcastle classification, measured using Cohen's Kappa statistic
articles.take_home: The new Suthersan classification system for midfoot injuries shows much better agreement between different doctors (0.95 versus 0.65) and stronger consistency when the same doctor reviews cases again (97.7% versus 87.9%) compared to the older classification system. This new system may help doctors predict outcomes and standardise treatment once it is tested in prospective studies.

articles.title: Injury and trauma care training in global health: A scoping review of costs and cost-effectiveness across resource settings.
articles.authors: Phan HT, Nguyen BT, Zhao T, Phung LT, Keakabetse TR, Dinh TTN, et al.
articles.population: Injury and trauma care training programmes across low-, middle- and high-income countries
articles.outcomes: Implementation costs and cost-effectiveness of trauma care training programs
articles.take_home: Trauma care training programmes were generally cost-effective, with costs ranging from USD 3.70 to USD 1433.50 per participant depending on the programme type. Using local trainers rather than foreign instructors and training-of-trainers models reduced costs by up to 28% without reducing training quality.

articles.title: Mechanism of injury not associated with acute compartment syndrome in Tibia fractures.
articles.authors: Egbe S, Marks D, Christiano AV, Erdman MK, Wolf JM, Strelzow J
articles.population: Skeletally mature patients with tibial fractures treated at a single level 1 trauma centre
articles.outcomes: Incidence of acute compartment syndrome (ACS) and associations with injury mechanism, fracture location, morphology and patient characteristics
articles.take_home: Acute compartment syndrome occurred in 2.1% of tibial fractures overall, with similar rates between blunt (2.7%) and ballistic (1%) injuries, meaning the type of injury mechanism did not predict ACS risk. However, patients with associated blood vessel injuries had significantly higher ACS rates regardless of how the injury occurred.

articles.title: Psychological outlook and recovery after orthopaedic trauma: Evidence and implications for Central India and other low-resource settings.
articles.authors: Salphale YS, Saodekar H, Kimmatkar N, Shinde G, Kothari P, Salphale AY
articles.study_type: Narrative review of observational and interventional studies
articles.population: Adults with orthopaedic trauma, with emphasis on low- and middle-income countries including India
articles.outcomes: Pain, disability, functional recovery, quality of life, rehabilitation adherence, and psychological distress (depression, anxiety, post-traumatic stress)
articles.take_home: Psychological factors like catastrophising and fear-avoidance worsen recovery after bone and joint injuries, while optimism and social support improve outcomes. Adding simple psychological screening and culturally appropriate support to trauma care could help patients recover better in low-resource settings.

articles.title: Peterson vs. Salter Harris physeal fracture classification systems: How consistent are they?
articles.authors: Charles-Lozoya S, Cobos-Aguilar H, Valdez-Cordova C, de la Parra-Marquez ML, Manilla-Munoz E, Arriaga-Cazares HE, et al.
articles.population: Paediatric orthopaedic surgeons and orthopaedic residents
articles.outcomes: Intraobserver and interobserver agreement for Salter-Harris versus Peterson physeal fracture classification systems
articles.take_home: Both classification systems showed similar reliability with moderate-to-substantial agreement. There were no significant differences between the Salter-Harris and Peterson classifications in how consistently doctors could classify growth plate fractures from radiographs.

articles.title: Do reduced screws have a significant impact on fixation in medial open wedge high tibial osteotomy? A comparative fatigue and stability analysis.
articles.authors: Ozkaya M
articles.population: Medial opening wedge high tibial osteotomy fixation models
articles.sample_size: Three fixation models (one standard, two modified)
articles.outcomes: Stiffness, deformation, tibial plateau displacement, stress levels, and fatigue life under centric, anterior and posterior loading
articles.take_home: Using two screws instead of three in high tibial osteotomy plates showed similar stability and strength under most loading conditions, though fatigue life was slightly reduced under backwards loading. Two screws may be a viable option that preserves more bone and reduces surgery time, though further refinement is needed.

articles.title: Moving beyond mortality measurements after road traffic injuries: Ten-year death risk after road traffic injuries.
articles.authors: Cherchiglia ML, Mougin V, Gray A, Wool EE, Malta DC, Melo APS, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study using record-linkage methodology
articles.population: People hospitalised for road traffic injuries in Brazil, aged 15 years and over, compared with those hospitalised for other reasons
articles.outcomes: All-cause and cause-specific mortality risk over 3 months to 10 years; relative risk of death for RTI patients versus other inpatients
articles.take_home: People who were hospitalised for road traffic injuries had significantly higher death rates over the following years compared to those hospitalised for other reasons, with particularly high risks in young and middle-aged men. Those with prior road traffic injuries also had increased risks of dying from causes including substance use, violence and suicide.

articles.title: Overutilization of helicopter emergency medical services compared to ground transport in moderate-severe penetrating abdominal trauma & associated outcomes.
articles.authors: Hus A, Brown A, Bundschu I, Prashar S, Kumar S, Indorewala Y, et al.
articles.population: Adults aged 18 years and over with moderate-to-severe penetrating abdominal injuries in hemorrhagic shock requiring massive transfusion protocol
articles.outcomes: Primary outcome: mortality at 24 hours and in-hospital. Secondary outcomes: transfusion requirements, intervention rate, and complications.
articles.take_home: Ground transport was associated with higher 24-hour mortality than helicopter transport, but when scene time was under 10 minutes, outcomes were similar between the two transport methods. This suggests helicopter transport may not always be necessary for fast ground transport situations.

articles.title: Early postoperative anxiety is associated with patient-reported recovery after hip fracture: A PROMIS-based longitudinal cohort study.
articles.authors: Achebe C, Cureton R, Rothberg D, Higgins T, Marchand L, Haller J
articles.population: Adults aged over 55 years with hip fracture treated with surgery
articles.sample_size: 304 patients for physical function outcome, 285 patients for pain interference outcome
articles.outcomes: Achievement of minimal clinically important difference in physical function and pain interference within one year after surgery
articles.take_home: Patients with higher anxiety levels in the early weeks after hip fracture surgery were less likely to achieve meaningful improvements in physical function and pain during the first year of recovery. Early anxiety appears to be a marker of recovery risk and targeting anxiety may help improve outcomes.

articles.title: Association between neighborhood opportunity and incidence of pediatric trauma.
articles.authors: Monroy A, Tan JM, Arbogast H, Spurrier R, Nager A, Chaudhari PP
articles.population: Children under 18 years old with home zip codes in Los Angeles County
articles.outcomes: Trauma activations per 100,000 children; injury mechanisms, severity and disposition by neighbourhood opportunity score
articles.take_home: Children living in neighbourhoods with fewer resources had higher rates of serious injuries like being hit as pedestrians or cyclists, firearm injuries and assaults, whilst children in better-resourced areas had more falls and sports injuries. This suggests injury prevention programmes should be tailored to the specific risks facing each neighbourhood.

articles.title: Satisfaction with nursing care among Jordanian burn patients admitted to burn critical care units: A cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Al-Ghabeesh S, Alnaeem M, Qaddoura N, Ali BA, Al-Masalha HA
articles.population: Adult burn patients admitted to a burn intensive care unit in Jordan
articles.outcomes: Patient satisfaction with nursing care measured using the Arabic Patient Satisfaction with Nursing Care Quality Questionnaire
articles.take_home: Burn patients in intensive care reported moderate satisfaction with nursing care. Higher income and younger age were associated with greater satisfaction, whilst patients with facial deformity reported lower satisfaction.

articles.title: Flexible intramedullary nailing in the treatment of paediatric forearm fractures: 15-years' experience at a level one trauma centre, comparison of implant retention versus removal and cost-effectiveness analysis.
articles.authors: Ismayl G, Alabbas A, Howard A, Fishlock A, Sabouni MY
articles.population: Children with forearm fractures treated with flexible intramedullary nailing
articles.outcomes: Incidence of secondary surgery, reasons for surgery, time to implant removal, patient and parent satisfaction, cost-effectiveness
articles.take_home: Leaving the metal nails in place after children's forearm fractures heal is safe and does not increase the need for further surgery compared with removing them. Keeping the nails in is also more cost-effective for the NHS.

articles.title: Association of Primary Trauma Care training with mortality in isolated traumatic brain injury: A sub-analysis of the PISTACHIO study in Vietnam.
articles.authors: Nguyen BT, Lam TT, Vu NHN, Pham TH, Do TD, Nguyen TTN, et al.
articles.population: Patients with isolated traumatic brain injury at two provincial hospitals in Vietnam
articles.outcomes: 24-hour and 30-day mortality rates
articles.take_home: Primary Trauma Care training was associated with a significant reduction in early deaths from isolated brain injury, with 24-hour mortality falling from 6.7% to 1.8% and 30-day mortality from 10.8% to 4.1%. The training appeared to help most in severe cases, where early 24-hour death rates dropped from 48.1% to 17.5%.

articles.title: Cemented versus uncemented femoral stem fixation in hip arthroplasty for displaced femoral neck fractures in elderly patients: A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.
articles.authors: Malki AT, Babtain HW, Alharbi DM, Shahhatalsayed AA, Alshaban RM, Almaghrabi YT, et al.
articles.study_type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials
articles.population: Elderly patients with displaced femoral neck fractures undergoing hip arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Mortality, periprosthetic fracture, dislocation, and wound infection
articles.take_home: Cemented femoral stems were associated with lower mortality and substantially lower risk of fracture around the implant compared with uncemented stems, with no increase in dislocation or infection rates. Cemented fixation appears to be the safer option for elderly patients with femoral neck fractures.

articles.title: Impact of surgical timing on complications and mortality in periprosthetic hip fractures compared to native hip fractures.
articles.authors: Aerden LKP, Brouwers X, Sermon A, Ghijselings S, Vles G, Herteleer M, et al.
articles.population: Patients who had surgery for periprosthetic hip fractures (fractures around hip replacements), compared with a benchmark group of patients with native hip fractures
articles.sample_size: 217 periprosthetic hip fracture patients surgically treated at a university trauma centre
articles.outcomes: Time to surgery, reasons for surgical delay, complication rates, length of hospital stay, and mortality at 30, 60, 90 days and six months
articles.take_home: Periprosthetic hip fracture patients waited much longer for surgery than native hip fracture patients (median 43.6 hours versus 16.8 hours), mainly due to surgeon availability and logistical issues rather than patient factors. However, delayed surgery did not increase complications or reduce overall survival in periprosthetic fracture patients.

articles.title: Superior survival, hemodynamics, and metabolic recovery using PEG-20k-based low volume resuscitation in a polytrauma hemorrhagic shock model in rats.
articles.authors: Khoraki J, Payne C, Lust D, Mangino MJ, Liebrecht LK
articles.population: Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats weighing 250-400 grams
articles.sample_size: 4 rats per group (3 groups total, n=12)
articles.outcomes: Survival time, blood pressure (mean arterial pressure), blood lactate levels, and amount of blood loss
articles.take_home: A new type of intravenous fluid called PEG-20k helped rats survive much longer after severe bleeding and injury compared to standard fluids, whilst maintaining better blood pressure and clearing acid from the blood more effectively. This shelf-stable fluid could potentially be useful for treating major trauma in settings where blood transfusions are not available.

articles.title: The forequarter lateral implosion injury: A diagnostic paradigm and Ch-Sh (chvarepsilon-sha) classification.
articles.authors: George A, Paull TZ, Winter JD, Levine J, Cole PA
articles.population: Patients with two or more rib fractures and shoulder girdle injury from trauma
articles.outcomes: ICU admission, pneumothorax, pulmonary contusion, mechanical ventilation, spinal or orthopaedic fractures, Injury Severity Score, 30-day mortality
articles.take_home: A new classification system (Ch-Sh) can help predict how severely patients with combined chest wall and shoulder injuries will be affected. More extensive chest wall injuries were linked with worse outcomes, but surprisingly, shoulder injuries that affect multiple structures may have better outcomes because the force is spread across more areas rather than concentrating damage to vital organs.

articles.title: More than meets the eye: the continuing importance of tertiary trauma survey even in moderate (ISS 9-15) trauma.
articles.authors: Knight C, Burke JR, Hind S, Smith SR
articles.population: Major trauma patients aged over 16 years with Injury Severity Score of 9 or more, admitted to a UK Major Trauma Centre
articles.outcomes: Incidence of unrecognised injuries identified at tertiary trauma survey; sensitivity and specificity of the survey; risk factors for unrecognised injuries
articles.take_home: Nearly one in five major trauma patients had injuries that were not spotted during initial assessments but found at a later detailed examination. This thorough third-stage survey was very good at finding these missed injuries, especially to the head and eyes, even in patients with less severe injuries.

articles.title: Minimal Movement Restrictions Do Not Increase Hip Dislocations Following Total Hip Arthroplasty: A Before-and-After Study of 10,357 Patients.
articles.authors: Klaassen AD, Willigenburg NW, Musters JWQ, Jager J, Poolman RW
articles.population: Patients who underwent total hip arthroplasty between January 2015 and March 2020
articles.sample_size: 10,357 patients (7,666 with strict restrictions, 2,691 with minimal restrictions)
articles.outcomes: Hip dislocation within 90 days after surgery
articles.take_home: Patients who followed a protocol with minimal movement restrictions after hip replacement did not have more hip dislocations than those with strict movement restrictions. This suggests that looser restrictions may be safe and could help patients return to normal activities more quickly.
